candidate_problems.text: How can 'relational genetic information' be made into a precise, testable theory? The conjecture claims DNA's informational content depends on an interpreter system (cell state, chromatin, maternal factors, spatial organization, environment), but it leaves open what the units, boundaries, and quantitative measures of that larger inherited developmental system are. A key unresolved tension is whether this is merely a richer metaphor or a framework that yields novel predictions beyond standard gene-regulatory-network and developmental-systems accounts.

What is the causal division of labor between genome-encoded constraints and non-genomic inherited structure in generating organismal complexity? The conjecture says complexity resides in the organization of the whole developmental system, with the genome as a 'non-sovereign component,' but it does not specify when genomic changes dominate outcomes versus when pre-existing cellular/developmental organization does. This raises a sharp open problem: how to partition explanatory and causal responsibility across DNA sequence, epigenetic state, maternal provisioning, cell architecture, and environmental inputs across evolutionary and developmental timescales.

What evolutionary mechanism explains the accumulation and stabilization of these historically layered control systems without collapsing into vagueness? The conjecture proposes that genomes were recruited into older continuity-preserving systems and later became embedded in layered regulation, but it remains unclear how such layers originate, become heritable, and are selected as integrated systems. The open question is whether there is a constrained evolutionary account of transitions from early hereditary chemistry to modern multilevel developmental control that explains observed patterns in genome size, regulatory complexity, and robustness.
